readiness_rationale: AC-1 (hash with argon2) rests on a verified finding and can ship now, but AC-3 (reject expired tokens) is blocked: the token verifier reads the system clock directly, so the expiry path is untestable. Not ready until a time seam exists. One step is therefore policy_candidate, not ready.

grounded: led-0009 (verified, grep src/users): UserService already hashes with argon2 - AC-1 oracle is reachable by wiring the existing hasher.
led-0007 (verified, npm test auth.spec.ts green): short-lived JWT + refresh chosen and a happy-path token round-trips.
tentative: led-0008 (assumed, contract AC-4): refresh endpoint must be rate-limited - no oracle yet, do not build a ready step on it.
unknown: Whether the token verifier can observe a controllable clock - the contract needs AC-3 but the ledger is silent on a time seam.
blocking_uncertainties: No verified path to make AC-3's oracle (rejects-expired-token) reach the expiry branch.

blocked_components: AC-3 (expired tokens rejected): oracle `npm test -- auth.spec.ts::rejects-expired-token` stays red; the verifier calls Date.now() directly so a test cannot advance time to trigger expiry. No verified control relieves this.
reachability_audit.missing_capability: An injectable time source (clock seam) behind the token verifier. Change the design to inject `now()`; do not keep tweaking the expiry check - every tweak is unreachable from a test without the seam.

evidence: Step 'wire argon2 hasher' executed turn 15; verdict passed; AC-1 oracle flipped red->green.
Step 'add expiry check to verifier' executed turn 15; verdict failed; AC-3 oracle still red (test cannot reach the branch).
what_worked: Reusing the existing argon2 hasher (led-0009) closed AC-1 cleanly.
what_failed_or_went_stale: The expiry-check step assumed time was controllable in tests; the failed verdict contradicted that.
carry_forward: AC-1 is green - do not touch the hasher again.
avoid_repeating: Do not re-propose editing the expiry check until a clock seam exists - it is unreachable from a test.

candidate_steps.action: Introduce an injectable time source: add a `now()` dependency to the token verifier (default System clock, overridable in tests).
candidate_steps.expected_effect: No oracle flips yet; this unblocks AC-3. Existing green oracles (AC-1, happy-path) stay green.
stop_or_remeasure_if: the verifier signature cannot accept an injected clock without a wider refactor (re-synthesize)

candidate_steps.action: With the clock injected, write/adjust the AC-3 oracle to advance time past expiry and assert rejection, then implement the expiry branch.
stop_or_remeasure_if: AC-3 oracle stays red after the change
a new constraint surfaces about refresh-token rotation

recommended_next_action.action: Introduce the injectable time source behind the token verifier (the missing capability from the reachability audit).
recommended_next_action.why: AC-3 is provably blocked without a clock seam; acquiring this capability is the only path that makes the expiry oracle reachable. This is a design change, not a tweak.
stop_or_remeasure_if: injecting the clock forces a wider interface change than expected

discriminators_if_not_ready.question: Does the existing token verifier already expose a seam to override the clock?
discriminators_if_not_ready.candidate_action: grep the verifier module for an existing clock/time abstraction or constructor-injected dependency.
expected_outcomes: a seam exists -> AC-3 becomes reachable now; lift this plan toward ready
no seam -> the clock-injection step stands as the missing capability

open_questions: Is AC-4 (refresh rate-limiting, led-0008 assumed) in scope for this contract version or deferred?
